content: Near the top of the todo list is a code of conduct. But not being able to pin something is quite a big problem. Anyone know how other public channels handled this?
we can keep a code of conduct in some designated place, and say that people are responsible to see it and adhere. of course we'll edit the rules together as we go.
Like a separate site which we can send links to when necessary? That sounds like the best solution to me.
It sounds like maybe the least-worst solution. Lack of pinning is really a bit of a problem. (The other really important thing to pin is instructions for how to embed LaTeX)
if we have a clear main "Welcome" message, we can keep editing it with everything we need.

So it seems like you can bulk-rename an entire topic. So what I propose is to have a topic with a single message in it, then when it needs replacing just rename it to something like "code of conduct- old version" and then make a new one
I just did "pin stream" to general. do y'all see that or is it only on my client?
What did you pin there?
I don't see it I think.
Also don't think so
From googling, I think "pin stream" causes #general to stay at the top of the stream list. Which, unrelated, is probably something we want anyway

Anyway we need one of these, now Christian figured out how to fake pinning stuff. Happy to write one, but not before I sleep, and someone with more code of conduct-writing experience than me will probably do a better job. (Anyone have a functioning code of conduct from another place that we can outrageously steal?)

rather than writing a code of conduct from scratch, it'd probably be a better idea to find one that some large community is already using and adapt that — something tried and tested is always better :)
Some ideas could be taken from here: http://eugeniacheng.com/inclusivity/

Yes, it's very lucky to have something written by a category theorist. But I think a code of conduct needs be much more concrete than a manifesto for a group at the size we already have: if you do X, then consequence Y will happen to you. (And I completely agree that copying an existing one is much better)
https://www.contributor-covenant.org/
this one tends to be popular and provides incident response guidelines
Used by such famously pleasant and nontoxic communities as the Linux kernel developers?

Jules Hedges said:

Used by such famously pleasant and nontoxic communities as the Linux kernel developers?

Oh, don't get me started...
Anyway, sorry folks, I conveniently need to unwind and sleep now instead of doing some honest work on this, I'll leave it to you in time zones further west
hey, they eventually got Linus to knock it off didn't they? (probably not a glowing endorsement, but still).
Haskell uses it, and it covers everything except bad actors.
